title: Cadences
text: CadencesPerfect and Imperfect CadencesEarlier, we looked at two cadences: perfect ('finished') and imperfect ('unfinished').Here is the perfect/authentic cadence again in D major:And here is the imperfect/half cadence example played by John:Note it is only the final two chords that make the actual imperfect cadence, i.e. from D major to A major. There are more cadences than just the perfect and imperfect. Let's have a look at some others.Interrupted/Deceptive CadenceHere we are in D major, with a phrase that finishes on chord vi -- which is, of course, B minor.Compare this with the perfect/authentic cadence example:Note again that the first chord is played for context -- the actual cadence is the movement from V – vi.The minor key example in G minor:Compared to the minor key perfect/authentic cadence:Plagal CadenceA plagal cadence ends with some finality on chord I - on the tonic.  But it has a different feel to a perfect V -I cadence. Here is an example of a plagal cadence in popular music (note that the chords fall approximately with lyrics, as shown):Something else to think about: Remember the discussion of modes from Week 1?  Playing through an octave from G to G on the white notes of a keyboard produces a particular set of tones and semitones which we can call the Mixolydian mode. It is characterised as sounding major in modality, but features a distinctive flattened seventh.  As John showed, we can transpose modes (in the same way we have been transposing major and minor keys). The famous melody to this song by The Beatles follows the Mixolydian mode, but it’s a Mixolydian on E. For more on this very common progression (in rock and funk), have a look at this Mixolydian mini-montageOpens in a new tab!
